The Nikkei Stock Average rose 1.9% at 38432.98 on Thursday. (kosaku.narioka@wsj.com)

data.title: Tokyo May Consumer Prices Signal Rise in Japan-Wide Inflation
data.description:
By Megumi Fujikawa

TOKYO--Consumer inflation in the Tokyo metropolitan area picked up in May, signaling persistent price pressures nationwide.

Excluding fresh food, consumer prices in Tokyo rose 3.6% in May from a year earlier as food prices continued to rise, official data showed Friday. That compared with April's 3.4% increase and the 3.5% growth expected in a poll of economists by data provider Quick.

Tokyo CPI figures are considered an early indicator of nationwide trends.

The Bank of Japan recently revised its price forecasts, expecting U.S. tariffs to drag on economic growth.

However, it has also become more cautious about the effect of food inflation on overall consumer prices, keeping a particularly wary eye on surging rice prices.

"Now we are facing another round of supply shocks in the form of food price increases," BOJ Gov. Kazuo Ueda said this week.

Friday's data showed that Tokyo's food prices excluding fresh food rose 6.9% on the year in May.

While the BOJ expects food inflation to ease, careful attention should be paid on how higher food prices will affect the nation's underlying inflation, Ueda said.

Write to Megumi Fujikawa at megumi.fujikawa@wsj.com

Yomiuri: Nissan Offers Early Retirement to U.S. Workers
data.description:
By Taku Mukoyama

Yomiuri Shimbun Staff Writer

Nissan Motor Co., which is currently undergoing corporate restructuring, has started offering early retirement to U.S. employees in both white-collar and blue-collar positions, part of a plan that will ultimately see 20,000 total jobs eliminated worldwide, the carmaker said Wednesday.

The offers were made to employees doing administrative work, such as personnel affairs and planning, in addition to those working at a factory in Canton, Miss. Nissan has not revealed how many people are eligible.

This move comes in addition to previous measures, in which the company offered voluntary retirement to employees in the United States from April to September last year due to poor business performance.

Nissan's performance is rapidly deteriorating. In its consolidated financial results for the fiscal year ending March 31, which Nissan announced on May 13, the company reported a net loss of 670.8 billion yen.

In addition to the job cuts, the company is planning to eliminate seven factories worldwide.

Nissan has also decided to offer the option of early retirement to employees in Japan from July to August, the first time it has done so in 18 years. By speeding up domestic and international restructuring efforts, the company hopes to reduce related costs by 500 billion yen.

----

This article is from The Yomiuri Shimbun. Dow Jones Newswires and The Wall Street Journal were not involved in the creation of this content.

YDN-m0000117174-1

data.title: US-China tariff talks 'a bit stalled,' needs Trump, Xi input, Bessent says
data.description:
U.S. trade talks with China are "a bit stalled" and getting a deal over the finish line will likely need the direct involvement of President Donald Trump and Chinese President Xi Jinping, U.S. Treasury Secretary Scott Bessent said on Thursday.

Two weeks after breakthrough negotiations led by Bessent that resulted in a temporary truce in the trade war between the world's two biggest economies, Bessent told Fox News that progress since then has been slow, but said he expects more talks in the next few weeks.

"I believe we may at some point have a call between the president and party Chair Xi," Bessent said.

"Given the magnitude of the talks, given the complexity ... this is going to require both leaders to weigh in with each other," he said. "They have a good relationship, and I am confident that the Chinese will come to the table when President Trump makes his preferences known."

The U.S.-China agreement to dial back triple-digit tariffs for 90 days prompted a massive relief rally in global stocks. But it did nothing to address the underlying reasons for Trump's tariffs on Chinese goods, mainly longstanding U.S. complaints about China's state-dominated, export-driven economic model, leaving those issues for future talks.

Since the mid-May deal, the Trump administration has concentrated on tariff negotiations with other major trading partners, including India, Japan and the European Union. Trump last week threatened 50% tariffs on EU goods, only to delay that threat.

A U.S. trade court on Wednesday ruled that Trump overstepped his authority in imposing the bulk of his tariffs on imports from China and other countries under an emergency powers act. But less than 24 hours later, a federal appeals court reinstated the tariffs, saying it was pausing the trade court ruling to consider the government's appeal. The appeals court ordered the plaintiffs to respond by June 5 and the administration to respond by June 9.

Bessent said earlier that some trading partners, including Japan, were negotiating in good faith and that he detected no changes in their postures as a result of the trade court ruling. Bessent said he would meet with a Japanese delegation on Friday in Washington.

data.title: Aroa Biosurgery Target Price Raised 9.3% to A$0.82/Share by Wilsons>ARX.AU
UK Car Production Slumps 15.8% in April
data.description:
UK car production fell 15.8% year-on-year in April 2025 to 59,203 units, as US tariffs and the timing of Easter disrupted manufacturing.

Excluding 2020, when COVID-19 halted production, this marks the lowest April output since 1952.

The decline was attributed to multiple factors, including fewer working days due to the later Easter holiday, which also contributed to the comparatively stronger figures in March.

Additional pressures included model changeovers and weakening demand in key export markets.

Exports, which account for the majority of UK car production, dropped 10.1%, while domestic production fell 3.3%.

Shipments to the UK’s two largest overseas markets declined, with exports to the EU down 19.1% and to the US down 2.7%.

Nevertheless, the EU remained the top destination, receiving over half of all exports, while the US accounted for 16.5%.

In contrast, exports to China and Turkey saw strong growth, rising by 44.0% and 31.2%, respectively.

data.title: Tokyo Core CPI Accelerates to 3.6% in May; Headline Inflation Eases to 3.4%
Costco's tariff cure includes cheaper eggs, rerouted items and U.S.-made mattresses
data.description:
By Bill Peters

'There's probably not a lot that can come from this print that will change the market's perception that [Costco] is a good place to be,' UBS says

Costco Wholesale Corp. on Thursday reported fiscal third-quarter profit and revenue that topped Wall Street's expectations, but its same-store sales came in a bit light, as analysts zero in on the impact of tariffs on retailers' costs and consumer behavior.

The membership warehouse chain (COST) earned $4.28 a share during the quarter, with total revenue of $63.21 billion. Same-store sales were up 5.7%, with e-commerce sales up 14.8%.

Analysts polled by FactSet expected Costco to report adjusted earnings per share of $4.24 for its fiscal third quarter. They expected $63.13 billion in revenue. However, they also forecast a 6% increase in same-store sales, a bit higher than what Costco reported.

Shares of Costco were up fractionally after hours. Over the past 12 months, the stock has risen 23.7%.

Many of Costco's retail peers have tried to convey restraint on raising prices as they wrestle with President Donald Trump's tariffs. The harshest of those tariffs, announced in early April, are on hold for now, and a federal court on Wednesday blocked many of those duties, although an appeals court paused that decision on Thursday.

But heading into the results, analysts offered up a familiar refrain on the big-box chain: Its size is its advantage - with the suppliers it buys from as well as the customers it sells to - as both lower- and higher-income shoppers continue to seek out deals and navigate rising costs of living.

"There's probably not a lot that can come from this print that will change the market's perception that [Costco] is a good place to be," UBS analyst Michael Lasser said in a research note last week.

Lasser said Costco's "all-weather-type characteristics" would give it an advantage over most other retailers. He noted that the products Costco sells that are exposed to tariffs make up one-third of its U.S. business. The chain's exposure to imports from China was probably "less than 10% of its U.S. business," he said.

Lasser added that the company would probably use the current turmoil in the economy to keep prices low and solidify its position as a hub for value purchases.

"This should fuel its traffic and membership momentum, leading to continued market-share gains," he said.

-Bill Peters

This content was created by MarketWatch, which is operated by Dow Jones & Co. MarketWatch is published independently from Dow Jones Newswires and The Wall Street Journal.

data.title: Core inflation in Japan capital hits 3.6%, keeps BOJ rate-hike chance alive
data.description:
Key points:
  • Tokyo core CPI rises 3.6% yr/yr in May vs f'cast +3.5%
  • Index excluding fuel up 3.3% yr/yr in May
  • Data highlights sticky food inflation

Core inflation in Japan's capital hit 3.6% in the year to May, data showed on Friday, marking a more than two-year high in a sign persistent rises in food costs will keep the central bank under pressure to hike interest rates further.

The data highlights the dilemma the Bank of Japan (BOJ) faces in balancing mounting inflationary pressures and the hit to Japan's economy from steep U.S. tariffs.

The increase in the Tokyo core consumer price index (CPI), which excludes volatile fresh food costs, exceeded a median market forecast for a 3.5% gain and followed a 3.4% rise in April. It was the fastest annual pace of increase since January 2023, when it hit 4.3%.

A separate index that strips away the effects of both fresh food and fuel costs, closely watched by the BOJ as a broader price trend indicator, rose 3.3% in May from a year earlier after a 3.1% rise in March.

Part of the rise was due to the base effect of last year's sharp drop caused by the launch of school education subsidies and the phase-out of nationwide subsidies to curb utility bills.

But the data showed signs of sticky food inflation with non-fresh food prices up 6.9% in May from a year earlier. The price of rice soared 93.2% from year-before levels.

While uncertainty over U.S. tariffs will likely keep the BOJ on a holding pattern, the price pressure may not allow the bank to pause on rate hikes for too long, some analysts say.

BOJ Governor Kazuo Ueda said on Tuesday the central bank must be vigilant to the risk rising food prices could push up underlying inflation that is already near its 2% target.

The BOJ ended a decade-long, massive stimulus programme last year and in January raised short-term interest rates to 0.5% on the view Japan was on the cusp of durably meeting its 2% inflation target.

While the central bank has signalled readiness to raise rates further, the economic repercussions from higher U.S. tariffs forced it to cut its growth forecasts and complicated decisions around the timing of the next rate increase.

A Reuters poll, taken on May 7-13, showed most economists expect the BOJ to hold rates steady through September with a small majority forecasting a hike by year-end.

data.title: Nikkei May Fall as Tariff Uncertainty Continues — Market Talk
data.description:
1945 ET - Japanese stocks may fall as uncertainty over U.S. tariffs continues. Prospects of the Bank of Japan's continued tightening may also weigh on the market. Nikkei futures are down 1.4% at 37840 on the SGX. USD/JPY is at 143.80, down sharply from 145.68 as of Thursday's Tokyo stock market close. Investors are focusing on any developments related to tariffs and the BOJ's policy making. Government data showed earlier that the consumer price index for Tokyo, excluding fresh food, rose 3.6% on year in May, compared with a 3.5% rise expected. The Nikkei Stock Average rose 1.9% at 38432.98 on Thursday. (kosaku.narioka@wsj.com)

1628 ET - Two economists say the less-timely, but closely watched, Canadian nonfarm payrolls report for March is signaling a possible recession. Payrolls fell to a 10-month low after payrolls in March fell 54,100, or a third-straight month-over-month drop. National Bank Financial's Mathieu Arsenault says the data now indicate that over 70% of private-sector industries have shedding jobs. "Such broad-based diffusion of weakness has historically occurred only during recessions." David Rosenberg, head of Rosenberg Research, calls the payrolls data "a complete dud" that should compel the Bank of Canada to cut its interest rate next Wednesday, even though core inflation has accelerated above 3%. He adds the most domestically economic-sensitive sector, accommodation and food services, has faltered in the past 3 months. "There can be little doubt Canada has entered a recession," Rosenberg adds. (paul.vieira@wsj.com; @paulvieira)

1625 ET - Goldman Sachs economists add a caveat to the rise in initial U.S. unemployment filings last week: "Jobless claims readings can be more volatile in the weeks surrounding the Memorial Day holiday," they write. The rise in claims to 240,000 last week roughly matches the highest seasonally adjusted levels recorded so far this year, but economists will want to see a bit more evidence from the volatile data series before overreacting. The May jobs report due Friday June 6 will provide a more complete look at the labor market. (matt.grossman@wsj.com; @mattgrossman)

1551 ET - Treasury yields fall ahead of inflation data and as GDP and jobless claims data indicate the economy is cooling, but not too fast, supporting the Fed's wait-and-see stance. Bid-to-cover ratio in a seven-year Treasury auction increases, signaling resilient demand. April annual PCE inflation, due tomorrow, is expected to slow slightly to 2.2% from 2.3%, in a WSJ survey. The core reading is forecast to remain at 2.6%. The University of Michigan consumer survey is expected to fall to 51.5 from 52.2. The 10-year yield falls 0.055 percentage point to 4.423% and the two-year sheds 0.052 p.p. to 3.938%, in both cases the lowest yield since May 9. (paulo.trevisani@wsj.com; @ptrevisani)

1528 ET - The policy backdrop grew more complex this week as a trade court blocked much of President Trump's tariff agenda--before an appeals court paused that ruling. Meanwhile, the White House says Trump told Fed Chair Jerome Powell on Thursday he wants lower interest rates. Neither headline will budge the Fed from its wait-and-see stance for now, Fed watcher Kevin Burgett of LHMeyer writes, projecting the Fed won't cut rates again this year. "We see no impact on our policy call from the meeting Trump had with Powell," Burgett writes. The trade-war litigation too will likely keep the Fed waiting for more clarity. "Even if this decision stands--which it may not--this isn't the end of Trump's trade war but rather a complication," Burgett writes. (matt.grossman@wsj.com, @mattgrossman)

1519 ET - In introductory remarks at a Fed conference in Washington Thursday, Federal Reserve governor Adriana Kugler says she remains attentive to a slow-burning story in the financial world: risks from commercial-real-estate mortgages extended to struggling properties. In many places, office buildings and other commercial venues haven't recovered from the changes Covid-19 wrought on the economy. Faltering debt threatens some banks and other lenders, Kugler says. "As we have seen in past crises, such as the Global Financial Crisis, vulnerabilities in specific sectors can have far-reaching consequences for the financial system," she cautions. (matt.grossman@wsj.com; @mattgrossman)

1505 ET - Elon Musk is leaving DOGE, President Trump's tariff agenda is in jeopardy and the House passed a reconciliation bill projected to increase deficits. The year so far has been a "missed opportunity" for Republicans--in control of the White House and both chambers of Congress--to put the U.S. on a better fiscal path, AllianceBernstein economist Eric Winograd tells The Wall Street Journal. He notes that President Trump won't face another election, which could have freed him to make challenging calls to slash spending, a path not taken by the White House so far. "This was an opportunity to make progress on fiscal policy that was politically unpopular," Winograd says. (matt.grossman@wsj.com, @mattgrossman)

1501 ET - Initial jobless claims in the latest week rose to one of the highest seasonally adjusted levels so far this year, the Labor Department says. Eric Winograd, an economist at AllianceBernstein, suspects the numbers may be understating the problem. "I have serious questions about whether that data set is useful anymore," Winograd tells The Wall Street Journal. He is concerned that especially amid a broad decline in union membership, many workers may not be aware they are eligible for unemployment after losing their jobs and decline to file. "Between people's awareness of their eligibility, and their efficiency at collecting the claims to which they're entitled, there's a gap," Winograd surmises. (matt.grossman@wsj.com; @mattgrossman)

1444 ET - The shadow monetary-policy panel at Canadian think tank CD Howe Institute recommends the Bank of Canada cut its policy interest rate next Wednesday by a quarter point, to 2.5%, and one additional quarter-point reduction to 2.25% by the end of the year. Six of the 9 panelists recommended the cut, even though recent inflation data indicates that underlying, or core, CPI climbed above 3%, or the upper end of the BOC's inflation-targeting range. The majority of panelists say the weakening employment data — including nonfarm payrolls at a 10-month low — are an indicator that "demand is flagging and a disinflationary output gap is opening up." Those arguing for no change in rates say BOC officials need to wait until inflation decelerates. (paul.vieira@wsj.com)

1302 ET - Mortgage rates rose to 6.89% this week, posing significant challenges for homebuyers and keeping the "lock-in effect" in place, says Realtor.com senior economist Jake Krimmel. According to Freddie Mac the 30-year mortgage rate rose 3 basis points to its highest level since Feb. 6. "With rates threatening to cross 7% for the first time since January," Krimmel says, "the 'lock-in effect'--where current homeowners are reluctant to sell and give up their lower mortgage rates--shows no signs of easing up." According to a recent Realtor.com survey, just 2% of respondents said they would be willing to buy with rates above 6%. But new listings and total homes for sale continued to rise last month, and homes are staying on the market for longer. Buyers may be regaining some bargaining power despite rising interest rates. (chris.wack@wsj.com)

1145 ET - The U.S. economy is likely to remain weak in the 2Q, as activity is weighed down by tariff uncertainty, Comerica Bank's Bill Adams write. First-quarter GDP growth estimate improved slightly to a negative 0.2% from minus 0.3%. Adams braces for another slow quarter. A court ruling against most Trump tariffs will likely prompt importers to increase their orders to get ahead of potential price increases, he says. "There are signs that consumers have been a little more cautious toward discretionary spending recently, too." Adams expects growth to pick again in the 2H "as the economic narrative refocuses away from tariff hikes and toward tax cuts." (paulo.trevisani@wsj.com)

1051 ET - Millennials are the only generation with a greater interest in buying a home in the next six months compared to last September, according to Realtor.com. Millennials' buying intention grew to 23% this year compared to last September's 15%, although most Americans--69%--aren't planning to engage in a real estate transaction in the next six months. High mortgage rates continue to impact the market. Younger generations are particularly affected, with over half of Millennials and Gen Z stating they've postponed their purchase. Gen Z appears to be the most cautious and wary of high mortgage rates, showing an increased inclination toward renting. A majority--57%--across all generations have used personal savings to finance their home purchase, while 15% have accessed personal investments or retirement accounts. (chris.wack@wsj.com)

Gold Edges Lower; May be Supported by Weak U.S. Economic Data — Market Talk
data.description: Gold edges lower in the early Asian session on possible position adjustment. However, the gold price may be supported by weak U.S. economic data released overnight. Both initial claims and continuing claims suggest a potential weakening in the labor market, Pepperstone's Quasar Elizundia says in an email. Also, confirmation of a contraction in GDP for 1Q, albeit with a slight upward revision, solidified perceptions of an economic slowdown, the research strategist says. "The combination of labor market stress and economic contraction, even amid the slight GDP revision, sets the stage for gold to regain prominence," Elizundia says. Spot gold is 0.1% lower at $3,314.76/oz. (ronnie.harui@wsj.com)

data.description:
Investors are likely to keep an eye on the following small-cap stocks listed on the Australian Securities Exchange.

West Coast Silver

West Coast Silver started diamond drilling at its Elizabeth Hill project in Western Australia to target extensions to the known silver mineralization and investigate structural controls identified through recent geological modelling and fieldwork.

Lunnon Metals

Lunnon Metals found a new gold structure at the Kambalda project in Western Australia during recent reverse circulation drilling aimed at sterilizing the areas surrounding the potential development footprint at the Lady Herial deposit.

Platina Resources

Platina Resources will start air-core drilling programs at its Challa and Beete projects in Western Australia in June to follow up on gold intersections and anomalies from previous drilling.

Pantera Lithium

Pantera Lithium welcomed the Arkansas Oil and Gas Commission's approval of a 2.5% mineral owner minimum lithium royalty structure for brine-based lithium projects in the Smackover Formation.

Rumble Resources

data.title: SEC Dismisses Lawsuit Against Binance — WSJ
data.description:
By Alexander Osipovich

The Securities and Exchange Commission dismissed its lawsuit against Binance and its billionaire founder Changpeng Zhao, the agency's latest move to wind down Biden-era enforcement actions against the crypto industry.

The details

In a Thursday court filing, attorneys for the SEC, Binance and Zhao signed on to the agency's decision to dismiss the 2023 lawsuit. The filing noted that the SEC recently created a "crypto task force" aimed at crafting a new regulatory framework for digital assets, part of a broader effort by the Trump administration to create a friendlier environment for the industry.

"We're deeply grateful to Chairman Paul Atkins and the Trump administration for recognizing that innovation can't thrive under regulation by enforcement," a Binance spokeswoman said in a statement, referring to the SEC's new leader.

The context

When the SEC sued Binance in June 2023, it was led by Gary Gensler, a Biden appointee who argued that the crypto markets were riddled with scams and needed to be subjected to the same laws that govern securities such as stocks and bonds. Gensler's SEC sued an array of big crypto firms, including Binance, the world's largest digital-currency trading platform.

In its lawsuit, the SEC alleged that Binance operated unregistered securities exchanges, misrepresented trading controls and commingled investors' assets with company funds. Binance has always said the case was without merit.

Binance separately pleaded guilty that year to violating U.S. anti-money-laundering rules and agreed to pay a $4.3 billion fine. Zhao served four months in jail on a related charge.

The big picture

Crypto is riding high in the U.S. right now, boosted by the favorable regulatory environment. Bitcoin's price is up more than 50% since President Trump's election victory, after recently hitting a record high, and Vice President JD Vance addressed a cheering audience at a bitcoin conference this week.

The SEC's dismissal of its Binance lawsuit is the latest sign that the Trump administration is focused on cooperating with the industry. It also comes as the president and his family have faced criticism over their private crypto business interests.

The Wall Street Journal reported in March that Zhao had been seeking a pardon while representatives of the Trump family held talks to invest in the U.S. arm of Binance. Zhao and Binance denied the talks, although Zhao has confirmed seeking a pardon.

Write to Alexander Osipovich at alexo@wsj.com

data.title: JAPAN MAY TOKYO CORE CPI (EX-FRESH FOOD) +3.6% Y/Y (APR +3.4%, FEB +2.4%), MEDIAN FORECAST +3.5% (RANGE: +3.3% TO +3.6%)
